title: Asukas First Name
wikitext:
[]: Asuka's First Name?             Quote Post by narcosis219 » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:11 am Is Asuka's first name Asuka? cuz her name is written as Asuka Langley and Asuka Soryu Langley... and I don't know if its in the Japanese order or western.

[]:             Quote Post by jcmoorehead » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:44 am Yeah her first name is Asuka.

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:54 am "Asuka" is most definately her first name. The real confusion comes wit her surname, since it's kind of convoluted. She gets the name "Langley" from her American father, but for some reason she also inherited the maiden name of her maternal grandmother, "Sohryu". I don't know how it works in other cultures, but typically I assumed that children take on the surname of their father, with the full married surname of the mother being kind of unusual. With Asuka's mother, she faced a similar plight, with her name being "Kyoko Zepplin Sohryu", since she was a German and Japanese mix. Why Asuka inherited "Sohryu" instead of "Zepplin" from her mother though, I do not know. I think the creators just wanted an excuse to giver her a more Japanese sounding name to refer to her by, since some Japanese people get annoyed by western surnames. Also, you'll sometimes see her reffered to as "Sohryu Asuka Langley", since in Japan it's more common to write the surname before the first name, like "Ikari Shinji", but Langley comes at the end since that's more common in the west. GAH! Even more convolution!

[]:             Quote Post by Faußtin » Tue Aug 01, 2006 2:14 pm ??? I'm totally confused. Which one is her family name? Her "first name" is Asuka, as far as I know. If we know which one is her family name, our problem would be solved. Because: "family name"-Asuka-"remaining name" is the "structure" as I far as I know. Which means nothing... I'm not familiar how the Japanese order of names.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Aug 01, 2006 2:54 pm FauÃŸtin wrote: I'm totally confused. Which one is her family name?

[]:             Quote Post by Legend of the Past » Tue Aug 01, 2006 3:05 pm Odd, I mean Asuka Langley seems to be written as a middle name. Russians have Middle Names, too, and they are named after the father. Both Male and Female are usually reffered to by both Name and Middle name, Like 'Roman Abramovich' (My name), mostly to seperate me from any other possible Roman in the known universe.

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:08 pm Mr. Tines wrote: FauÃŸtin wrote: I'm totally confused. Which one is her family name?

[]:             Quote Post by tinmeigut » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:20 pm Well Timstuff explained that pretty well. It's just a difference in culture. In China and Japan you put your surname first, you want people to know what clan you are from. In the Western part, you let people know your own name first. The reason for Soryu Asuka Langley is used but not Soryu Langley Asuka is that you can't have two surname, except after you've been married to your husband. Then in Chinese culture you may have two surnames. Asuka is till single, and there's no reason for her to have two surname, it's just strange in Asian culture. So she must give up her maiden name, and used Soryu as her surname.

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:27 pm I can't really think of any kids who are born with two surnames though, unless they were born of two parents who were so full of pride that they couldn't agree on which last name to give them (I'm guessing this happens in Hollywood a lot). Adding even further to the confusion, why was Asuka's mother reffered to as Kyoko Zepplin Sohryu, and not Kyoko Zepplin Langley, or Kyoko Sohryu Langley? Does that mean when Asuka gets married, she'll end up with three surnames, or will she have to drop one of her existing ones? And which would it be, the one from her father, or the one from her mother? It's so confusing, and it's highly irregular for an un-married person to have two surnames in any of the cultures cultures I know of, ontop of the fact that most married women don't even have two. In western culture, the maiden name is usually dropped after marraige, and in eastern culture it's usually the same, unless the husband decides to take the wife's name instead (which is uncommon there, but socially acceptable). I really do think that it's all just a cop out, otherwise they'd never have created such a convoluted scenario.

[]:             Quote Post by tinmeigut » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:45 pm Well in the old Chinese generation married women did have two surnames after they married. Let me take an example, if a lady's surname was Chan, and her husband's was Wang, then her name after marriage would be Wang Chan something.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE 08 » Tue Aug 01, 2006 10:47 pm Sounds like Vladmir Kaganovich needs a middle name....

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Tue Aug 01, 2006 11:33 pm tinmeigut wrote: Well in the old Chinese generation married women did have two surnames after they married. Let me take an example, if a lady's surname was Chan, and her husband's was Wang, then her name after marriage would be Wang Chan something.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Aug 02, 2006 1:16 am The Sohryu name would be handed down as the last "real" (i.e. Japanese) surname on the family tree. Kyoko was a scientist, and thus likely for professional reasons would have kept her name unchanged after marriage. That's if Langley hadn't already divorced her before death, and her name was a reversion to the original form. In the UK, at least, it is not uncommon that if the wife's name is unusual in some respect, that the two surnames be hyphenated together, especially if the husband's is very common (e.g. Smith). This is before we get on to odd modern things like making a mash-up of the surnames.

[]:             Quote Post by Legend of the Past » Wed Aug 02, 2006 3:35 am SEELE 08 wrote: Sounds like Vladmir Kaganovich needs a middle name....

[]:             Quote Post by Eternal Yamcha » Wed Aug 02, 2006 4:09 am ...Shit, I could almost be Asuka's dad... Or uncle or uhm... Cousin... Odds are the whole "Zepplin" thing is like... Her father coming from a western culture, but that'd cut Asuka's heritage to some weird ass number Japanese... *Shrugs* I have no real explanation, I've seen Asuka's name spelled out "Sohryu Asuka Langley" which would hint that "Langley" is a sort of middle name... But I doubt that that's it. It's probably just a bit of a hole there and she probably just adopted the name to hide her western heritage, same with Kyoko... Since their first names are Japanese. All I know is her name would be "Asuka Langley" in western cultures and thus, we'd share the same last name.

[]:             Quote Post by Legend of the Past » Wed Aug 02, 2006 6:00 am Wikipedia claims she's three-quarters German, one quarter Japanese.

[]:             Quote Post by Faußtin » Wed Aug 02, 2006 7:15 am Well, we Hungarians also put the family name to the first place, then may come everything else. For example, John Steven Smith would be like Smith John Steven. Logic: family name, first name, second name, and so on. If John would like to introduce himself, he'd say "Hi, I'm Smith John". But if it is required for some official stuff, then he would have to tell all his names. He could tell them to others when introducing himself, though many think that is firstly inconvenient, secondly people can barely remember two simple names, not more. Perhaps Japanese do it like this too, though this is just a guess. End of story. So, my point is that I think that her name should be spelled as Sohryu Asuka Langley or Asuka Langley Sohryu. The way she introduces herself matters on her, since both are correct in a certain way. But I wonder which one is the original... since the one she chooses decides wether she should be considered European or Japanese. But tell me: why is it that the English version switches the order of names? If it is originally Japanese, it should be used as they use it, and the case should be the same if the name is originally English. But perhaps its just me. I tend to overreact a couple of things. This is one of them.

[]:             Quote Post by Eternal Yamcha » Wed Aug 02, 2006 7:52 am But tell me: why is it that the English version switches the order of names?

[]:             Quote Post by Faußtin » Wed Aug 02, 2006 8:39 am Eternal Yamcha wrote: But tell me: why is it that the English version switches the order of names?

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Wed Aug 02, 2006 10:07 am Legend of the Past wrote: Wikipedia claims she's three-quarters German, one quarter Japanese.

tin » Wed Aug 02, 2006 7:15 am Well, we Hungarians also put the family name to the first place, then may come everything else. For example, John Steven Smith would be like Smith John Steven. Logic: family name, first name, second name, and so on. If John would like to introduce himself, he'd say "Hi, I'm Smith John". But if it is required for some official stuff, then he would have to tell all his names. He could tell them to others when introducing himself, though many think that is firstly inconvenient, secondly people can barely remember two simple names, not more. Perhaps Japanese do it like this too, though this is just a guess. End of story. So, my point is that I think that her name should be spelled as Sohryu Asuka Langley or Asuka Langley Sohryu. The way she introduces herself matters on her, since both are correct in a certain way. But I wonder which one is the original... since the one she chooses decides wether she should be considered European or Japanese. But tell me: why is it that the English version switches the order of names? If it is originally Japanese, it should be used as they use it, and the case should be the same if the name is originally English. But perhaps its just me. I tend to overreact a couple of things. This is one of them.
